title: Excitation density controlled regimes of collective light--matter dynamics
abstract: Theoretical descriptions of collective light--matter dynamics often rely on the mean-field (MF) or single-excitation (SE) approximations, yet the parameter regimes where they apply are rarely clearly delineated. Here we show that representative limiting regimes are characterized by two independent parameters: the number of molecules $N$ and the excitation number $N_{\rm exc}$. In the Tavis--Cummings model, when $N\gg 1$ and the excitation density $N_{\rm exc} / N \to 0$, MF and SE descriptions agree and yield linear collective dynamics, showing harmonic Rabi oscillations. At finite excitation density ($N_{\rm exc} / N \sim \mathcal{O}(1)$), the large-\(N\) limit remains accurately described by MF dynamics but becomes nonlinear in $N_{\rm exc} / N$, manifested by a Duffing equation for the cavity amplitude with anharmonic Rabi frequency. We further show that cluster expansion systematically restores finite-$N$ correlations beyond MF. When local vibronic interactions are included, the same linear collective limit is reached by both approximations, with SE reaching it through polaron decoupling and MF through linearization. This two-parameter regime map clarifies the limits in which different theoretical descriptions provide controlled descriptions of collective light--matter dynamics.
summary: This paper identifies that the validity of common approximations in collective light-matter dynamics depends on both the number of molecules and the excitation density. It demonstrates that while the large-N limit can be described by mean-field theory, high excitation densities introduce nonlinearities described by a Duffing equation. The work provides a clear map to guide researchers in selecting appropriate theoretical models for molecular polaritons.
main_problem: The lack of clear delineation regarding the parameter regimes where mean-field (MF) and single-excitation (SE) approximations are valid for describing collective light-matter dynamics.
main_result: The authors establish a two-parameter regime map (based on molecule number N and excitation density n0) that identifies when MF and SE descriptions agree, diverge, or exhibit nonlinear dynamics.
method: Theoretical analysis using the Tavis-Cummings and Holstein-Tavis-Cummings models, supplemented by cluster expansion to restore finite-N correlations and the use of the Duffing equation to describe nonlinearities.
model_or_system: A collection of N identical two-level molecules coupled to a single cavity photon mode, including local harmonic vibrations (Holstein-Tavis-Cummings model).
key_observables: Photon number dynamics, molecular population dynamics, two-molecule correlations, and effective Rabi frequency.
important_parameters: Number of molecules (N), excitation density (n0 = N_exc/N), light-matter coupling strength (g_c), and vibronic coupling strength (c_v).
main_assumptions: The agreement between MF and SE is specific to the joint limit of large N and low excitation density; the study focuses on the bright-sector dynamics.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the molecular ensemble and the topology of the HTC model; Figure 3 shows photon number dynamics using the cluster expansion hierarchy; Figure 5 is a TOC graphic summarizing the core concept.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of approximation validity, defines the two-parameter regime map using the Tavis-Cummings model, extends the analysis to include vibronic interactions via the HTC model, and concludes with the implications for various spectroscopic and dynamical applications.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental diagnostic framework for choosing between semiclassical and quantum-mechanical descriptions in cavity QED and polaritonic systems, specifically clarifying how excitation density drives the transition to nonlinear dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The HTC model and dynamical regime map. (a) Schematic of a molecular ensemble coupled to a single cavity mode. (b) Coupling topology: the cavity mode cou- ples collectively to electronic transitions with single-molecule strength gc (collective Rabi splitting Ω= 2gc √

FIG. 2. Photon number dynamics |α(t)|2 of the TC model (cν = 0) calculated with MF for N = 1, 2, 4, 8, 104 with fixed α0 = 1, so Nexc ≡|α0|2 = 1 and n0 = 1/N decreases with increasing N. The large-N (small-n0) limit converges to the harmonic SE Rabi oscillation (dashed line), demonstrating the linear collective regime (N ≫1, n0 ≪1). Anharmonic Duffing dynamics is evident at small N (large n0).

FIG. 3. Cluster-expansion dynamics and finite-N corre- lations. (a) Photon number dynamics n(t) from identity- constrained CE hierarchy (colored curves) for N = 1, Nexc = 1, initialized from |1⟩⊗|g⟩. The MF curve (black dots) uses the coherent-field counterpart with the same initial mean pho- ton number, |α0|2 = 1. Vertical dashed lines mark the har- monic/SE Rabi period, T = 20.68 fs. FIG. 4. Convergence of the bright vibrational mode dynam- ics. Here we take ν = Ω= 0.2 eV and cν = 20 meV. (a) Vibra- tionally excited population PB1(t) + PC1(t) for N = 1, 2, 4, 8 calculated in the SE approximation. (b) Same as (a), for N = 10, 103, 105, 107, convergence reached as N = 107 (black curve). (c) MF b-channel population |ceb(t)|2/n0 for decreas- ing n0 (colored curves) converges to the SE large-N bench- mark (red dashed curve), demonstrating MF–SE agreement in the linear collective regime.
